past_medical_history: Migraine without aura diagnosed at age 17, approximately 2-3 episodes per month
Generalized anxiety disorder diagnosed at age 20
Iron deficiency anemia treated at age 22, resolved with supplementation
Episode of vertigo lasting 3 days at age 23, attributed to vestibular neuritis by PCP, resolved spontaneously

medications.frequency: As needed for acute migraine (uses 3-4 times per month)

family_history: Mother age 52 with migraine headaches and hypothyroidism
Father age 54 with type 2 diabetes and hypertension
Maternal grandmother had lupus, died at age 68 from renal complications
No family history of multiple sclerosis or other demyelinating diseases

social_history.exercise: Practices yoga 3 times per week, runs 2-3 miles occasionally
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in an apartment near campus

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented x4. Affect slightly anxious. MMSE 30/30. Normal attention, concentration, and recall. Speech fluent without paraphasic errors. No word-finding difficulty.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II-XII grossly intact. Visual acuity 20/20 bilaterally with correction. No RAPD. Visual fields full to confrontation. Fundoscopic exam unremarkable, no papilledema or disc pallor. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation and strength symmetric. Hearing grossly normal. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius full strength. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout. No pronator drift. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups bilaterally in upper and lower extremities. No fasciculations.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. No intention tremor. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal gait and tandem gait. Able to walk on heels and toes without difficulty.
neurological_exam.additional: Lhermitte sign not tested (no clinical indication). No Uhthoff phenomenon reported. Patient reports mild persistent fatigue but attributes this to graduate school stress and poor sleep.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of dizziness and fatigue, currently admitted for workup after an episode of transient right-hand clumsiness
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Elena Vasquez is a 25-year-old right-handed woman who presents to the neurology service after being admitted through the emergency department for evaluation of episodic neurological symptoms. She reports that over the past 18 months, she has experienced three to four episodes of dizziness described as a spinning sensation lasting anywhere from a few hours to several days, each time resolving on its own. She has attributed these to stress from her graduate program and her known history of migraines, although she notes the vertigo episodes are distinct from her typical headache pattern. Four days ago, she noticed clumsiness in her right hand while typing her dissertation, describing difficulty coordinating fine movements of her fingers that lasted approximately 36 hours before gradually improving. She also reports intermittent tingling in her feet over the past several months, which she assumed was related to sitting cross-legged while studying. She endorses significant fatigue that has worsened over the past year, often needing to nap in the afternoon despite sleeping 7-8 hours at night. She denies any visual changes, weakness, bowel or bladder dysfunction, heat sensitivity, or difficulty with balance. Her primary care physician had previously ordered basic bloodwork during one of her vertigo episodes, which was unremarkable. She was referred to ENT who diagnosed possible vestibular migraine and recommended vestibular rehabilitation, which she attended for four sessions without significant improvement. She was admitted after her PCP became concerned about the new onset of hand clumsiness and ordered an MRI of the brain which showed nonspecific white matter changes, prompting neurology consultation. She is anxious about the workup, worried that something serious might be wrong but also hoping this is all related to stress and her migraines.

findings.location: Left periventricular region, adjacent to the body of the lateral ventricle

additional_observations: No juxtacortical lesions with typical ovoid morphology
No spinal cord lesion visible on available imaging
Ventricles normal in size and configuration
Normal flow voids in major intracranial vessels
mri.impression: Five scattered T2/FLAIR hyperintense foci in the supratentorial white matter, predominantly subcortical in distribution. No lesion enhancement is identified. The findings are nonspecific and could represent small vessel ischemic changes, migraine-related white matter changes, or early demyelinating disease. The lesion distribution is not classic for multiple sclerosis as there are no typical periventricular Dawson finger morphology lesions, no infratentorial involvement, and no enhancing lesions to suggest active inflammation. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Hematocrit 35.6 % (L), MCV 79.8 fL (L), Sodium 127 mEq/L (L).

special_tests.oligoclonal_bands: Negative — no unique CSF-restricted oligoclonal bands detected (matched serum and CSF pattern)

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 14 cmH2O. WBC: 2 cells/uL (reference: 0-5) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 32.5 mg/dL (reference: 15-45 mg/dL). Glucose: 58.1 mg/dL (reference: 40-70 mg/dL) (ratio 0.67 (serum glucose 92 mg/dL)). oligoclonal_bands: Negative — no unique CSF-restricted oligoclonal bands detected (matched serum and CSF pattern). IgG_index: 0.58 (reference: 0.28-0.66, normal). myelin_basic_protein: 1.2 ng/mL (reference: <4.0 ng/mL, normal). CSF_cytology: No malignant cells identified.

output.impression: Abnormal values: Interocular latency difference 10 ms ms (H).

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord at C3-C4 level, dorsolateral

additional_observations: No leptomeningeal enhancement along the cord
Conus medullaris is normal in position and signal
Lesion does not span more than one vertebral segment (short-segment lesion)
output.impression: Single short-segment T2 hyperintense lesion in the dorsolateral cervical spinal cord at C3-C4 without enhancement or cord expansion. The dorsolateral location and short-segment morphology (less than 2 vertebral segments) are typical of multiple sclerosis rather than neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder, which typically produces longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis spanning 3 or more segments. No active enhancement suggests the lesion is chronic. Clinical correlation recommended.
Abnormal values: Average RNFL thickness 78 um um (H), Temporal quadrant RNFL 48 um um (H), Ganglion cell layer thickness 68 um um (H).

findings.location: Left periventricular region, adjacent to the body of the lateral ventricle
Right periventricular region, perpendicular to the lateral ventricle with ovoid morphology

additional_observations: Two new lesions identified compared to prior MRI from 3 months ago
Incomplete ring enhancement of the new periventricular lesion suggests active demyelination
Previously identified lesions are stable in size and appearance
The cervical cord lesion at C3-C4 (if reimaged) remains stable
output.impression: Follow-up MRI demonstrates two new white matter lesions compared to the study from 3 months ago: (1) a 7 mm enhancing right periventricular lesion with ovoid morphology perpendicular to the ventricle (Dawson finger morphology) showing incomplete ring enhancement, and (2) a 4 mm non-enhancing left juxtacortical parietal lesion. These new lesions provide evidence of dissemination in time (new lesions on follow-up) and the periventricular lesion with classic morphology strengthens evidence of dissemination in space. The combination of periventricular, juxtacortical, and spinal cord involvement with dissemination in time now fulfills the 2017 McDonald criteria for the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis. Clinical correlation recommended.

results.title: Diagnosis of multiple sclerosis: 2017 revisions of the McDonald criteria
results.authors: Thompson AJ, Banwell BL, Barkhof F, et al.
results.summary: The 2017 McDonald criteria allow CSF-specific oligoclonal bands to substitute for dissemination in time, and permit symptomatic and asymptomatic lesions for DIS and DIT criteria.

results.title: MRI criteria for multiple sclerosis: MAGNIMS consensus guidelines
results.authors: Filippi M, Rocca MA, Ciccarelli O, et al.
results.summary: Defines DIS as involvement of at least 2 of 4 CNS regions: periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord. DIT requires new T2 or enhancing lesions on follow-up OR simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions.

output.summary: The 2017 McDonald criteria for MS diagnosis require demonstration of dissemination in space (DIS) and dissemination in time (DIT). DIS requires lesions in at least 2 of 4 characteristic CNS regions. DIT can be demonstrated by new lesions on follow-up MRI, simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, or CSF-specific oligoclonal bands (new in 2017 revision). Importantly, the 2017 criteria allow CSF oligoclonal bands to substitute for DIT even in the absence of follow-up MRI evidence, which was a major change from the 2010 revision.

interactions: Topiramate: Both may cause lymphopenia; monitor CBC more frequently when co-administered
warnings: GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, abdominal pain) are common, especially in first month
Monitor CBC with differential every 6 months — discontinue if lymphocyte count <500 cells/uL for >6 months
Risk of progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) with prolonged lymphopenia
Flushing is common — may be mitigated by taking with food or pre-treatment with aspirin 325 mg
output.formulary_status: Tier 3 — specialty pharmacy, requires prior authorization
alternatives: Glatiramer acetate (Copaxone) — injectable, lower efficacy but well-established safety
Teriflunomide (Aubagio) — oral, teratogenic (contraindicated if pregnancy planned)
Ocrelizumab (Ocrevus) — high-efficacy, IV infusion every 6 months

differential.likelihood: Initially most likely given migraine history and nonspecific MRI
differential.key_features: Subcortical lesion distribution, no enhancement, normal CSF, young woman with established migraine diagnosis
Excluded by negative AQP4 antibodies; short-segment spinal cord lesion and subcortical brain lesions are atypical for NMOSD
Excluded by negative MOG antibodies; MOGAD typically presents with prominent optic neuritis with disc edema
Normal inflammatory markers, normal CSF cell count, no vascular territory pattern on MRI; would expect CSF pleocytosis and elevated protein
No leptomeningeal enhancement, normal inflammatory markers, no systemic symptoms or pulmonary findings

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history focusing on prior neurological episodes that may represent prior demyelinating events (vertigo, sensory symptoms, fatigue)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: History reveals episodic vertigo and transient sensory symptoms over 18 months that may represent prior relapses attributed to other diagnoses

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination including fundoscopy, visual acuity, and assessment of subtle pyramidal and sensory signs
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Examination is largely normal in this diagnostic puzzle, which is part of the difficulty — normal exam does not exclude MS

optimal_actions.action: Review brain MRI with attention to lesion morphology, distribution, and enhancement pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Nonspecific white matter lesions, predominantly subcortical, no classic Dawson fingers, no enhancement — atypical for MS but cannot exclude it

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI of cervical and thoracic spine with contrast to evaluate for spinal cord lesions (dissemination in space)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Short-segment cervical cord lesion at C3-C4, dorsolateral, consistent with demyelinating plaque

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands and IgG index
optimal_actions.expected_finding: CSF is normal including absent oligoclonal bands — this occurs in 5-10% of MS and is part of the diagnostic challenge

optimal_actions.action: Order visual evoked potentials to assess for subclinical optic nerve involvement
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Prolonged P100 latency in left eye suggesting subclinical optic nerve demyelination, providing additional evidence of dissemination in space

optimal_actions.action: Order optical coherence tomography to assess for RNFL thinning
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left eye RNFL thinning consistent with prior subclinical optic neuritis, corroborating VEP findings

optimal_actions.action: Order NMO-IgG (aquaporin-4) and MOG antibody testing to exclude mimics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Both antibodies negative, excluding NMOSD and MOGAD

optimal_actions.action: Order basic labs (CBC, BMP, ANA, B12, TSH) to exclude metabolic and autoimmune mimics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All labs normal except borderline ANA 1:40 which is nonspecific

optimal_actions.action: Plan follow-up MRI brain with contrast at 3 months to assess for dissemination in time
optimal_actions.expected_finding: New enhancing periventricular lesion with Dawson finger morphology and new juxtacortical lesion — confirms DIT and fulfills McDonald 2017 criteria

optimal_actions.action: Initiate disease-modifying therapy after confirmed diagnosis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Discuss treatment options; check for drug interactions with current medications

critical_actions: Obtain MRI of the spine to assess for spinal cord lesions as part of dissemination in space evaluation
Order visual evoked potentials to detect subclinical optic nerve demyelination
Test NMO-IgG and MOG antibodies to exclude important mimics before labeling as MS
Plan follow-up MRI to demonstrate dissemination in time given absence of oligoclonal bands
Recognize that normal CSF (absent OCBs) does not exclude MS — occurs in 5-10% of confirmed cases
Reconsider prior episodes (vertigo, sensory symptoms) as possible prior demyelinating events
contraindicated_actions: Dismissing white matter lesions as definitively migraine-related without further workup
Starting disease-modifying therapy before confirming the diagnosis with McDonald criteria
Performing brain biopsy for these nonspecific lesions without exhausting non-invasive diagnostic testing
Starting high-dose corticosteroids without clear evidence of active relapse
Attributing all symptoms to anxiety without thorough neurological evaluation
key_reasoning_points: This case is a diagnostic puzzle because the initial MRI findings are nonspecific and overlap significantly with migraine-related white matter changes, which is the most common initial misdiagnosis
The normal neurological exam and absent oligoclonal bands in CSF create diagnostic uncertainty, as many clinicians rely heavily on these for MS diagnosis
The key to solving this case is systematic evaluation for dissemination in space through spine MRI (cervical cord lesion) and VEP (subclinical optic nerve involvement)
Prior episodes of vertigo and sensory symptoms represent unrecognized relapses that were attributed to vestibular migraine and positional habits
The 2017 McDonald criteria allow CSF OCBs to substitute for DIT, but since OCBs are negative here, follow-up MRI is essential to demonstrate DIT
The follow-up MRI at 3 months reveals new enhancing and non-enhancing lesions with classic MS morphology (Dawson fingers), confirming the diagnosis
Negative NMO-IgG and MOG antibodies are essential to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD, which have different treatment approaches and prognosis

metadata.diagnostic_delay_typical: Months to years due to nonspecific initial presentation
